refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain teleoperation communication channels with end-to-end latency (sensor-to-operator-to-actuator) not exceeding 150 ms at the 95th percentile under agreed network conditions, enabling safe and responsive remote equipment control.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness and control responsiveness. The p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion is a hard constraint for safe remote operation. Refinement clarifies that latency is measured end-to-end (not just network transit), includes both uplink and downlink paths, and is conditioned on agreed network assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit verification and traceability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency under representative network conditions (agreed bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Conduct operator acceptance testing with simulated remote scenarios. Establish continuous monitoring of p95 latency in field deployment. Validate that latency remains within bounds across all operational modes and network conditions.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: Acquire sensor data (position, orientation, video, proximity) and transmit to remote operator with minimal delay.
FR-NGHE-008.2: Receive operator control commands and transmit to vehicle actuators with minimal delay.
FR-NGHE-008.3: Manage network communication protocol and buffering to minimize latency variance and jitter.
FR-NGHE-008.4: Monitor and report teleoperation latency metrics to enable real-time performance visibility.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement edge-based teleoperation architecture with local sensor aggregation, predictive buffering, and direct network transmission to minimize processing hops.
DP-B: Deploy cloud-based teleoperation with centralized command processing and latency compensation via predictive control algorithms.
DP-C: Hybrid architecture combining edge sensor acquisition with optimized network protocol (UDP-based, low-overhead serialization) and operator-side command buffering.
DP-D: Implement dedicated low-latency network overlay (MPLS, QoS-prioritized channels) with local sensor-to-network gateway and command-to-actuator gateway.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid architecture combining edge sensor acquisition with optimized network protocol (UDP-based, low-overhead serialization) and operator-side command buffering. This balances latency minimization (edge processing avoids cloud round-trip), operational flexibility (hybrid allows graceful degradation), and cost-effectiveness. UDP reduces protocol overhead vs. TCP; low-overhead serialization (e.g., Protocol Buffers, MessagePack) minimizes payload size; operator-side buffering absorbs network jitter.

potential_sources: DP-C couples to network infrastructure design (must support UDP and QoS tagging).
DP-C couples to sensor module design (must support edge aggregation and timestamping).
DP-C couples to operator interface design (must handle buffered command input and latency feedback).
DP-C couples to safety services (latency monitoring must feed safety decision logic).
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are manageable via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link). No circular dependencies detected. DP-C avoids tight coupling to cloud infrastructure, reducing operational risk.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) remain within agreed bounds. Risk: Unexpected network degradation could violate latency SLA; mitigation: continuous monitoring and fallback to reduced-fidelity telemetry.

Assumption: Edge devices (vehicle) have sufficient compute capacity for sensor aggregation and timestamping. Risk: Underpowered edge hardware could introduce processing delays; mitigation: hardware specification and performance testing.

Assumption: Operator interface can handle asynchronous, buffered command input without degrading usability. Risk: Operator confusion or delayed response; mitigation: HMI design review and operator training.

Assumption: UDP packet loss is acceptable and handled by application-layer retry logic. Risk: Critical commands lost in transit; mitigation: selective TCP for safety-critical commands, redundant transmission.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly specify: (1) UDP vs. TCP decision per message type (telemetry: UDP with retry; safety commands: TCP or redundant UDP); (2) serialization format and maximum payload size; (3) edge buffering strategy (ring buffer, priority queue); (4) operator-side latency compensation algorithm (predictive rendering, command extrapolation). Add acceptance criterion: 'Latency monitoring shall detect and alert on p95 > 120 ms (20 ms margin to 150 ms SLA)'.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns end-to-end teleoperation latency performance. Encompasses sensor-to-network gateway (edge), network protocol stack (UDP/TCP, serialization, buffering), and network-to-operator interface (command reception and latency feedback). Responsible for latency monitoring, jitter management, and graceful degradation under network stress.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module outputs sensor telemetry (position, orientation, video, proximity, timestamps) to operator. Latency of this interface is critical to acceptance criterion.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives operator control commands (movement, tool actuation, mode changes) via Tasking interface. Latency of command reception and transmission to Vehicle is critical.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module reports latency metrics and network health status via SafetyAlert. Safety Services may trigger latency-based safety actions (e.g., autonomous stop if latency exceeds threshold).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module interfaces with underlying network infrastructure (WAN, LTE, satellite, etc.) via Network Link. Responsible for protocol adaptation and QoS negotiation.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level container. Teleoperation is a major subsystem but not the entire solution. High diffusion: teleoperation latency affects all remote operation scenarios. Not recommended as IM boundary; too coarse.

Vehicle owns edge sensor acquisition and actuator control. Teleoperation latency depends on Vehicle's sensor-to-network gateway performance. However, Vehicle does not own operator-side buffering or network protocol. Partial containment; not recommended as sole IM boundary.

Teleoperation module is purpose-built for remote operation. Owns sensor-to-operator communication pipeline, network protocol, buffering, and latency monitoring. Minimal diffusion: latency concerns are localized to this module. Recommended as IM boundary.

Safety Services owns safety decision logic and alerts. Teleoperation latency is an input to safety decisions (e.g., trigger autonomous stop if latency exceeds threshold), but Safety Services does not own the latency mechanism itself. Not recommended as IM boundary.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of end-to-end latency performance. It encompasses the sensor-to-operator communication pipeline, network protocol optimization, buffering strategies, and latency monitoring. Ownership is clear: Teleoperation team is accountable for meeting the p95 ≤ 150 ms criterion. Interfaces to Vehicle (sensor data), Safety Services (latency alerts), and Network Link (transport) are well-defined and non-overlapping.
containment_risks: Risk: Vehicle's sensor acquisition and timestamping performance directly impacts Teleoperation latency. Mitigation: Define sensor interface contract (e.g., 'Vehicle shall timestamp sensor data within 10 ms of acquisition') and include Vehicle performance in Teleoperation acceptance testing.

Risk: Network Link performance (jitter, packet loss) is outside Teleoperation's direct control. Mitigation: Establish network SLA (agreed conditions) and design Teleoperation to gracefully degrade if SLA is violated. Monitor and alert on network health.

Risk: Operator interface design (HMI responsiveness, command buffering) affects perceived latency. Mitigation: Teleoperation module must provide latency feedback to operator interface; coordinate with operator interface team on buffering strategy.

Risk: Safety Services may impose latency-based safety constraints (e.g., 'if latency > 120 ms, reduce speed'). Mitigation: Teleoperation and Safety Services must align on latency thresholds and safety actions during design phase.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly define Teleoperation module interfaces: (1) Sensor Input Interface (from Vehicle): timestamp, data format, max latency budget; (2) Command Output Interface (to Vehicle): command format, transmission guarantee (UDP vs. TCP per message type); (3) Latency Monitoring Interface (to Safety Services): latency metrics, alert thresholds; (4) Network Interface (to Network Link): protocol, QoS requirements. Establish latency budget allocation: e.g., 'Vehicle sensor acquisition: 10 ms, edge buffering: 20 ms, network transit: 80 ms, operator-side processing: 30 ms, margin: 10 ms = 150 ms total'.

fr_dp_gate.reason: Refined FR clearly specifies the latency requirement (p95 ≤ 150 ms, end-to-end, under agreed network conditions). Selected DP-C (hybrid edge + optimized network protocol) directly addresses this requirement via edge processing (reduces round-trip latency), UDP (reduces protocol overhead), and operator-side buffering (absorbs jitter). DP-C is traceable to FR and provides a logical solution path. No ambiguity or mismatch detected.

diffusion_risks: DP-C depends on Vehicle's sensor acquisition performance. Mitigation: Define sensor interface contract and include Vehicle in latency budget allocation.
DP-C depends on Network Link's QoS capabilities (UDP support, low jitter). Mitigation: Establish network SLA and design graceful degradation if SLA is violated.

DP-C depends on operator interface design (command buffering, latency feedback). Mitigation: Coordinate with operator interface team; Teleoperation module provides latency metrics via SafetyAlert interface.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module has high containment (0.95) for DP-C. Owns sensor-to-operator communication pipeline, network protocol, buffering, and latency monitoring. External dependencies (Vehicle, Network Link, Safety Services) are well-defined via interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link). No diffusion of latency responsibility across multiple modules. Ownership is clear and accountable.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Latency requirement is measurable and testable. Verification approach: (1) Lab testing with simulated network conditions (agreed bandwidth, jitter, packet loss); (2) Field testing under representative operational scenarios; (3) Continuous monitoring in deployment (p95 latency metrics logged and analyzed). Acceptance criterion is objective (p95 ≤ 150 ms). Verification is feasible and cost-effective.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Latency requirement is allocated to Teleoperation module with clear ownership. Sub-requirements (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.4) decompose the latency requirement into sensor acquisition, command transmission, protocol management, and monitoring. Latency budget allocation (Vehicle: 10 ms, edge: 20 ms, network: 80 ms, operator: 30 ms, margin: 10 ms) provides clear targets for each component. Allocation is clear and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Refine latency budget allocation to include specific targets for each component (Vehicle sensor acquisition, edge buffering, network transit, operator-side processing). Establish acceptance criteria for each component.

Define network SLA (agreed conditions) explicitly: bandwidth, jitter, packet loss, availability. Specify Teleoperation behavior if SLA is violated (e.g., reduce telemetry fidelity, trigger safety alert).
Establish latency monitoring and alerting strategy: continuous p95 measurement, alert thresholds (e.g., p95 > 120 ms triggers warning, p95 > 150 ms triggers safety action), logging and analysis.
Coordinate with Safety Services on latency-based safety actions: define thresholds, response times, and fallback behaviors (e.g., autonomous stop if latency exceeds 150 ms for > 5 seconds).
Establish interface contracts between Teleoperation and Vehicle, Network Link, Safety Services: data formats, timing guarantees, error handling.

decision.justification: Refined FR-NGHE-008 is clear, measurable, and safety-critical. Selected DP-C (hybrid edge + optimized network protocol) is a sound logical solution with manageable couplings. Assigned IM (Teleoperation module) has high containment (0.95) and clear ownership. All gates pass. However, the requirement is not atomic and has identified sub-requirements (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.4) that require further decomposition. Latency budget allocation and interface contracts need refinement at the next level. Recommend decomposing sub-FRs and allocating latency targets to each component (Vehicle, edge, network, operator). This is a natural decomposition point.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: Acquire sensor data (position, orientation, video, proximity) and transmit to remote operator with minimal delay. [Allocated to Vehicle + Teleoperation edge gateway]
FR-NGHE-008.2: Receive operator control commands and transmit to vehicle actuators with minimal delay. [Allocated to Teleoperation + Vehicle]
FR-NGHE-008.3: Manage network communication protocol and buffering to minimize latency variance and jitter. [Allocated to Teleoperation]
FR-NGHE-008.4: Monitor and report teleoperation latency metrics to enable real-time performance visibility. [Allocated to Teleoperation + Safety Services]

revise.why: DP-C requires refinement to specify: (1) UDP vs. TCP decision per message type; (2) serialization format and payload size limits; (3) edge buffering strategy (ring buffer, priority queue); (4) operator-side latency compensation (predictive rendering, command extrapolation); (5) latency monitoring algorithm and alert thresholds. These details are necessary to guide implementation and verify latency performance.

assumptions: Network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) remain within agreed bounds throughout operation.
Vehicle has sufficient compute capacity for sensor aggregation and timestamping (< 10 ms latency).
Operator interface can handle asynchronous, buffered command input without degrading usability.
UDP packet loss is acceptable and handled by application-layer retry logic; safety-critical commands use TCP or redundant transmission.
Teleoperation module can achieve < 20 ms edge buffering and < 30 ms operator-side processing latency.
Network transit latency is < 80 ms under agreed conditions (e.g., LTE with < 50 ms RTT).
open_risks: Network degradation (unexpected jitter, packet loss, congestion) could violate latency SLA. Mitigation: continuous monitoring, graceful degradation, fallback to reduced-fidelity telemetry.
Vehicle sensor acquisition latency exceeds budget (> 10 ms). Mitigation: hardware specification, performance testing, latency budget review.
Operator interface design introduces latency (buffering, rendering). Mitigation: HMI design review, latency feedback to operator, operator training.
Safety Services imposes latency-based safety constraints that conflict with operational requirements. Mitigation: early alignment on latency thresholds and safety actions.
Network protocol overhead (serialization, framing) exceeds budget. Mitigation: protocol optimization, payload size limits, performance testing.
Latency monitoring introduces overhead that impacts latency performance. Mitigation: efficient monitoring algorithm, sampling strategy, separate monitoring path.
evidence_needed: Network SLA specification: agreed bandwidth, jitter, packet loss, availability profiles.
Vehicle sensor interface contract: timestamp accuracy, data format, max latency budget.
Latency budget allocation: detailed breakdown by component (Vehicle, edge, network, operator) with acceptance criteria.
DP-C refinement: UDP vs. TCP decision, serialization format, buffering strategy, latency compensation algorithm.
Latency monitoring design: algorithm, alert thresholds, logging strategy, integration with Safety Services.
Operator interface design: command buffering strategy, latency feedback mechanism, HMI responsiveness.
Lab test results: latency measurements under simulated network conditions, p95 latency validation.
Field test results: latency measurements under representative operational scenarios, operator acceptance feedback.
Safety Services alignment: latency-based safety thresholds, response times, fallback behaviors.
